message:
test(conformance): expand ext4/btrfs adversarial corpus + 9 new ext4 MUST clauses

Conformance + fuzz coverage push. ext4 jumps from 66 → 75 MUST clauses
(100% passing) and the adversarial corpus grows in two crates:

ffs-harness/tests/conformance.rs (+204 lines):
- Ext4InlineData read boundary (2 cases): reads crossing, at, and past
  EOF return exact bytes / empty output for inode-body and
  xattr-continuation storage.
- Ext4InlineData extreme read boundary (2 cases): zero-size and
  oversized/extreme-offset reads clamp safely for both storage classes.
- Ext4InlineData VFS surface (3 cases): mounted root readdir, lookup,
  getattr, path resolution, and readback through the looked-up inode.
- Ext4InlineData RW boundary (2 cases): read-compatible while
  write/fallocate mutation rejects with EOPNOTSUPP and preserves data.

ffs-ondisk/tests/adversarial_corpus.rs (+1276 lines): synthetic
adversarial seeds added across 7 surfaces, all wired into the
deterministic fuzz regression harness:

- Ext4 inline-data ibody xattrs: huge i_size, oversized i_extra_isize,
  ibody-xattr magic-only, name overflow, value overflow, valid smoke.
- Ext4 external xattr blocks: bad magic, header-only empty block, name
  overflow, value overflow, valid user-xattr smoke.
- Ext4 directory blocks: valid multi-entry checksum-tail iteration,
  short/unaligned/out-of-bounds rec_len, name overflow, nonzero
  checksum-tail padding.
- Ext4 extent trees: valid leaf and index nodes, bad magic,
  eh_entries > eh_max, truncated entries, overlapping leaf extents,
  unsorted index entries, and extent-block checksum stamping/corruption.
- Btrfs tree blocks: valid leaf and internal nodes, excessive tree
  level, leaf payload overlap with item table, payload out-of-block
  bounds, overlapping leaf payload ranges, zero child block pointers,
  tree-block checksum stamping/corruption.
- Btrfs sys_chunk_array: valid single-device bootstrap mapping, bad
  chunk key type/objectid, zero chunk length / stripe length / stripes,
  multiple RAID profile bits, truncated stripe data.
- Btrfs dev items: full field-layout parsing, max numeric values,
  trailing bytes after the fixed 98-byte item, truncated payload
  rejection.

conformance/COVERAGE.md updated to reflect new totals and check off the
seven adversarial-corpus next-actions; .beads/issues.jsonl flushes the
companion task transitions.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-6tme","title":"EPIC: Killer Demo — Self-Healing Durability","description":"# EPIC: Killer Demo — Self-Healing Durability

## PURPOSE
Create a compelling, CI-executable demonstration of FrankenFS self-healing: flip random bits in blocks, show automatic repair using RaptorQ symbols, and output a human-readable repair ledger explaining exactly what was reconstructed. This is the "show, don't tell" proof of FrankenFS's core innovation.

## DEMO SCRIPT

### Setup (automated)
1. Create 128MB ext4 image with FrankenFS (RaptorQ enabled, 5% overhead)
2. Write 100 files with known content (photos, text, binaries)
3. Record BLAKE3 checksums for all files
4. Print: "Filesystem created: 100 files, all checksums verified"

### Corruption (visible to audience)
5. Print: "Injecting corruption: flipping random bits in 3% of blocks..."
6. Corrupt 3% of data blocks with random bit flips
7. Print block IDs and bit positions of corrupted blocks
8. Attempt to read files — show which files have corrupted bytes

### Repair (the magic moment)
9. Print: "Triggering self-healing scrub..."
10. Run scrub daemon (or explicit repair command)
11. Show real-time progress: "Scanning block group 1/32... corruption detected at block 1042... repairing with 4 symbols... OK"
12. Print: "Repair complete in 2.3 seconds"

### Verification (proof)
13. Read all 100 files, verify BLAKE3 checksums
14. Print: "All 100 files verified — zero data loss"
15. Print repair ledger: table of every repair action with timing, blocks, symbols used

### Output Format
```
=== FrankenFS Self-Healing Demo ===
Created: 100 files (128MB image, 5% RaptorQ overhead)
Corrupted: 47 blocks across 8 block groups (3% corruption rate)

Repair Results:
  Block Group  Corrupted  Repaired  Symbols Used  Time
  1            3          3         4             12ms
  4            8          8         12            34ms
  7            6          6         8             28ms
  ...
  Total        47         47        72            180ms

Verification: 100/100 files checksums match — ZERO DATA LOSS
Evidence ledger: 47 CorruptionDetected + 47 RepairSucceeded records
```

## REQUIREMENTS
- Must run without root (FUSE user mount)
- Must complete in < 30 seconds
- Must be deterministic (seeded corruption)
- Must be a single command: `cargo run --bin ffs-demo -- self-healing`
- Must work in CI (no GUI, no interactive input)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Demo runs successfully with single command
- [ ] All corrupted blocks repaired
- [ ] Repair ledger is human-readable and correct
- [ ] Demo completes in < 30 seconds
- [ ] Runs in CI without root
- [ ] Output suitable for README/blog post

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. Demo runs with single command in < 30s
3. All corrupted blocks repaired (zero data loss)
4. Output suitable for README/blog post
5. CI-executable without root or FUSE","notes":"DEPENDENCY NOTE: This demo is the capstone of the self-healing story.  {"id":"bd-6tme.2","title":"Adoption wedge: deterministic MVCC snapshot isolation demo","description":"# Adoption wedge: deterministic MVCC snapshot isolation demo

## GOAL
Single-command demo showing MVCC snapshot isolation on a filesystem: two concurrent readers see consistent snapshots while a writer modifies files. The adoption wedge for the MVCC story.

## DELIVERABLES
1. `cargo run --example mvcc_demo` -- creates image, starts 2 read transactions + 1 write transaction, shows isolation
2. Output shows: reader A sees version 1, writer commits version 2, reader A still sees version 1, reader B (started after commit) sees version 2
3. No FUSE mount needed -- operates on raw image via API
4. Deterministic, < 3 seconds

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: demo exits 0 with expected output pattern
2. Integration: snapshot isolation invariant verified

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Transaction start (info): txn_id, snapshot_seq
- Read (debug): txn_id, block, version_seen
- Write commit (info): txn_id, commit_seq, blocks_written
- Isolation check (info): reader_txn, expected_version, actual_version, isolated=true/false

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Single command, < 3 seconds
2. Demonstrates snapshot isolation visually
3. Deterministic
4.  {"id":"bd-jtt2","title":"Implement external journal paired replay (not just UUID validation)","description":"BACKGROUND: ext4 filesystems can use an external journal device (JOURNAL_DEV incompat flag on the data FS, journal_dev field points to device number, journal_uuid links the pair). When crash recovery is needed, the JBD2 journal blocks live on the external device rather than in an internal inode.

CURRENT STATE: OpenOptions::external_journal_path accepts an external journal image path. maybe_probe_external_journal opens it, reads its JBD2 superblock, validates UUID pairing and block size match, and stores ExternalJournalInfo on OpenFs. But maybe_replay_ext4_journal short-circuits when journal_dev != 0 — it logs 'journal replay skipped' and returns Ok(None). No actual replay occurs.

The log message honestly states: 'external journal UUID validation only; full paired replay not yet implemented'.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:
1. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is Some AND UUIDs match:
   a. Open the external journal image as a ByteDevice.
   b. Parse the JBD2 superblock from the external device.
   c. Compute journal segments: the entire external device (minus superblock) is the journal area.
   d. Feed the segments to replay_jbd2_segments the same way we do for internal journals.
   e. The replay writes go to the DATA filesystem's device, not the journal device.
2. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is None AND crash recovery IS needed:
   - Currently returns UnsupportedFeature error (good — fail-fast).
   - No change needed here.
3. When journal_dev != 0 AND external_journal_path is None AND crash recovery NOT needed:
   - Currently returns Ok(None) with info log (good — graceful skip).
   - No change needed here.

KEY CONSIDERATIONS:
- The external journal's block layout is different from an internal journal. The JBD2 superblock is at block 0 of the device, and journal blocks start immediately after. There's no inode or extent mapping.
- We need to compute JournalSegment { start, blocks } for the external device based on the JBD2 superblock's s_maxlen field.
- The replay must write to the data device, not the journal device. The existing replay infrastructure takes a BlockDevice for writes — we just need to pass the data device.
- After successful replay, we should clear the needs_recovery flag on the data device's superblock.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- A test creates a synthetic data FS + external journal pair, dirties the journal, and verifies replay applies blocks to the data device.
- The log message changes from 'UUID validation only' to 'external journal replay completed'.
- UUID mismatch still fails fast.
- Block size mismatch still fails fast.

 {"id":"bd-z15u","title":"e2compr: implement single-indirect block pointer read-modify-write","description":"Implement write support for indirect block pointers (i_block[12] -> pointer block -> data). This is the first level of indirect addressing and covers logical blocks 12 through 12+ptrs_per_block-1 (for 4K blocks: 12 through 1035).

STEPS:
1. When logical_block >= 12 and < 12 + ptrs_per_block:
   a. Read i_block[12]. If 0, allocate a new block, zero-fill it, write i_block[12].
   b. Read the indirect block.
   c. Write the target pointer at the correct 4-byte offset within it.
   d. Write the indirect block back to disk.
2. For freeing: when all pointers in the indirect block become 0, free the indirect block itself and set i_block[12] = 0.
3. Update set_indirect_block_ptr to dispatch to this path when lb >= 12 && lb < 12 + ptrs_per_block.

ACCEPTANCE: Compressed write works for files up to 4MB (1024 clusters * 4 blocks * 4K = 16MB with shift=2).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:07.272650906Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:28.119620954Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:28.119131667Z","close_reason":"Added write_block_ptr() with single-indirect allocation + read-modify-write.
